title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: This PR introduces critical security vulnerabilities across nearly every new file: live production secrets are committed to the repository, two independent code-injection vectors exist (eval and new Function) reachable from unauthenticated/low-privilege query parameters, a hardcoded backdoor token grants unconditional admin access, MD5 is used for password hashing, full card numbers and CVCs are stored and then exported in plaintext CSV, a query-parameter auth bypass ships to staging, and webhook delivery is vulnerable to SSRF with no URL validation. The PR should not be merged in its current state.

findings.title: Production secrets committed to the repository
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and a live payment-gateway key are hardcoded in a .env file that is force-included in version control (see .gitignore change). Anyone with read access to the repo now has these credentials. Rotate all three values immediately, delete the file from git history (git-filter-repo or BFG), and add the path back to .gitignore without the negation. Secrets should be injected at runtime via a secrets manager or CI environment variables — never committed.

findings.title: .gitignore negation intentionally tracks .env with live secrets
findings.message: The comment reads 'keep ours checked in for staging' but the file contains PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY — a live key, not a staging one. Even staging secrets should not live in source control. Remove the `!.env` line and restore the original ignore rule.

findings.title: eval() executes attacker-controlled input — arbitrary code execution
findings.message: `evalWhere` builds a JavaScript expression from the raw SQL WHERE clause and passes it to `eval()`. The `listAllUsers(filter)` path in AdminService receives `filter` directly from `req.query.filter` with no sanitization. An authenticated admin can execute arbitrary Node.js code in the server process (e.g. `filter=1; require('child_process').execSync('...')`). The comment 'Yes, we know' does not make this acceptable. Remove eval entirely; implement a proper field-equality filter using a safe allowlist approach.

findings.title: new Function() executes attacker-controlled filter expression — RCE
findings.message: `listAllTransactions` calls `new Function('tx', 'return (' + filterExpr + ')')` where `filterExpr` is `req.query.filter` passed through with no validation. This is functionally equivalent to eval and provides a second, independent RCE vector. Replace with a safe filter DSL — e.g. allow only `{ field, op, value }` objects parsed from JSON, applied with explicit property access.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 grants unconditional admin access to any caller
findings.message: Any request to any protected route — including /admin/users and /webhooks — can skip authentication entirely by appending `?adminBypass=1`. This is deployed to staging (and possibly further). Remove this bypass completely; use a proper staging test account instead.

findings.title: Hardcoded backdoor token 'admin-master-key' grants permanent admin access
findings.message: Any caller presenting the literal string `admin-master-key` as their Bearer token receives a verified admin identity, bypassing all JWT signing. This is a hardcoded credential that cannot be rotated and that exists in git history forever. Remove the special-case block entirely.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing — trivially crackable
findings.message: MD5 is a general-purpose hash with no cost factor; a GPU can compute billions per second. Entire MD5 password databases are rainbow-table'd in minutes. Replace with bcrypt (cost ≥ 12), argon2id, or scrypt. All existing hashes must be re-hashed on next login.

findings.title: Full card number and CVC stored on transaction object — PCI DSS violation
findings.message: `card_number: input.card.number` and `cvc: input.card.cvc` are added to every persisted Transaction. PCI DSS explicitly prohibits storing CVCs after authorisation and restricts full PANs to isolated, encrypted vaults. Remove both fields from the transaction model immediately. The cardLast4 field is sufficient for display purposes.

findings.title: console.log dumps full transaction including card_number and CVC to logs
findings.message: `console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction))` will write the full card number and CVC to every log sink (stdout, log aggregators, etc.). Remove this log line. The structured pino logger call below already records the necessary transaction metadata safely.

findings.title: CSV export includes full card_number — PCI data exposed to admin download
findings.message: The `exportTransactionsCsv` header includes `card_number` and the row builder emits `tx.card_number`. This creates a downloadable file containing raw PANs, violating PCI DSS. Remove the card_number column from the export; cardLast4 is permitted.

findings.title: Registration logs raw request body — password written to logs in plaintext
findings.message: `console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body))` writes the user's plaintext password to every log sink. Remove the log line entirely; if debugging is needed, log only the email.

findings.title: password_hash returned in registration response body
findings.message: The register endpoint includes `password_hash: user.password_hash` in the JSON response. Even an MD5 hash leaks the ability to crack the password offline. Remove `password_hash` from the response; return only `{ id, email, role }`.

findings.title: Password reset token returned in HTTP response and logged — trivially hijackable
findings.message: `res.json({ ok: true, resetToken })` sends the reset token back to the caller, and `console.log` also writes it to logs. A reset token must only be delivered via a side channel (email). Returning it in the response means any XSS or log-access attack can steal it. Send by email only; never include it in the API response.

findings.title: SSRF: webhook URLs are fetched with no validation or allowlist
findings.message: Any registered webhook URL is fetched with `redirect: 'follow'`. An attacker can register `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (AWS IMDS), `http://localhost:6379` (Redis), or other internal addresses and receive their responses via timing/error side-channels or have the server perform unintended actions. Additionally `fireForTransaction` iterates ALL webhooks regardless of owner — one user's transaction fires every other user's webhook. Validate that URLs are https, public-IP-only (block RFC-1918 and link-local ranges), and fire only the webhook owner's hooks.

findings.title: JWT tokens signed with no expiry — tokens are valid forever
findings.message: `jwt.sign(payload, SECRET)` with no `expiresIn` option produces tokens that never expire. A stolen token grants permanent access. Add at minimum `{ expiresIn: '1h' }` and implement a refresh-token flow.

findings.title: CORS configured with wildcard origin and credentials: true
findings.message: `cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true })` is both insecure and non-functional: browsers reject credentialed requests to wildcard origins per the CORS spec. Specify an explicit allowlist of frontend origins instead.

findings.title: Login returns 404 when email not found — enables user enumeration
findings.message: Returning HTTP 404 for unknown email vs. 401 for wrong password lets attackers enumerate which email addresses have accounts. Return 401 with a generic 'invalid credentials' message in both cases.

findings.title: Password reset token uses Math.random() — not cryptographically secure
findings.message: `Math.random()` is a PRNG, not a CSPRNG. Its output can be predicted given enough observations. Use `crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex')` for reset tokens.

findings.title: Module-level webhooks array is shared across all WebhooksService instances
findings.message: The `webhooks` array is declared at module scope, not on the class instance. Every `WebhooksService` instance — including those created in separate test runs via `createApp()` — mutates the same array. This causes test pollution and would cause incorrect isolation in any multi-tenant deployment. Move the array inside the class as `private readonly hooks: Webhook[] = []`.

findings.title: Admin authorization check duplicated across all four controller methods
findings.message: Each of `listUsers`, `listTransactions`, `stats`, and `exportCsv` re-implements the same email-domain check, including the `adminBypass` escape hatch. The `requireAdmin` middleware in `authMiddleware.ts` already exists for this purpose but is not wired up on the admin routes in `routes.ts`. Remove the inline checks and add `requireAdmin` as a route-level middleware on all `/admin/*` routes.

findings.title: JSON body limit increased to 5 MB — increases DoS surface
findings.message: The previous limit was 64 KB which is appropriate for payment API payloads. Increasing it to 5 MB to support 'large CSV exports' is backwards: CSV generation happens server-side and the response is streamed out, so large request bodies are never needed. Revert to 64 KB or set the 5 MB limit only on specific admin import routes if they ever exist.

findings.title: Template-literal SQL in createUser/findUserByEmail risks injection if migrated to real DB
findings.message: String interpolation like `VALUES ('${id}', '${email}', ...)` and `WHERE email='${email}'` is the canonical SQL injection pattern. The stated goal is to 'swap in Postgres later with minimal churn at the call sites', meaning this code is likely to be copy-pasted into a real query. Parameterise from the start: use a placeholder array structure that maps to prepared statements.

findings.title: FakeDb DELETE ignores the WHERE clause and deletes all rows
findings.message: The DELETE branch does `this.users = []` unconditionally. Any query like `DELETE FROM users WHERE id='x'` will wipe the entire user table. Implement per-row filtering or, if delete is not yet needed, throw an error so misuse is caught early.

findings.title: findUserByEmail logs the raw SQL query including user-supplied email
findings.message: `console.log('userStore.findUserByEmail sql:', sql)` writes the constructed query (and thus the caller-supplied email address) to logs. This is PII and should not be logged. Remove the log line.

findings.title: Tests share global userStore state — second test may fail depending on run order
findings.message: Both test cases call `createApp()` which reuses the module-singleton FakeDb. The 'logs in an existing user' test registers bob, but if 'registers a new user' ran first and registered alice on the same db instance, there is no isolation. More critically, there are no tests for: the adminBypass, the filter injection vectors, the CSV export, the webhook registration, or the password-reset token leakage — all of which are the highest-risk new paths.
